message.content:
Decompose this research question into complementary search angles.

## Question
Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.

## Task
Generate 5 distinct web search queries that together cover the question from different angles. Pick angles that suit the question's domain. Examples:
- broad/primary  · academic/technical  · recent news  · contrarian/skeptical  · practitioner/implementation
- For medical: anatomy · common causes · serious differentials · authoritative refs · red flags
- For tech: state-of-art · benchmarks · limitations · industry adoption · cost/tradeoffs

Make queries specific enough to surface high-signal results. Avoid redundancy.
Return: the question (verbatim or lightly normalized), a 1-2 sentence decomposition strategy, and the angles.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze this research question about audio comprehensibility and speed listening, then decompose it into complementary search angles.

input.question:
Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.</question>
<parameter name="angles">[{"label": "broad/primary — time-scale modification algorithms", "query": "WSOLA PSOLA phase vocoder time-scale modification speech speed-up algorithm comparison intelligibility", "rationale": "Surfaces the core DSP techniques named in the question and how they preserve pitch while compressing duration, the foundation of speed-listening playback."}, {"label": "academic/technical — perceptual limits of accelerated speech", "query": "perceptual limits comprehension time-compressed speech maximum word rate intelligibility threshold psychoacoustics study", "rationale": "Targets peer-reviewed research on where comprehension breaks down (typically cited around 2.5-3x / ~275-400 wpm), grounding the 'limits' part of the question in evidence."}, {"label": "training/adaptation effects — blind and trained listeners", "query": "blind listeners ultra-fast speech screen reader adaptation training 16x 22x time-compressed speech comprehension neuroplasticity", "rationale": "The >16x claim comes largely from blind power-users of screen readers; this angle finds studies on training, cortical reorganization, and adaptation enabling extreme rates."}, {"label": "intelligibility enhancement & silence/pause compression", "query": "speech intelligibility enhancement silence pause removal spectral prosody processing podcast smart speed dynamic compression", "rationale": "Covers the comprehensibility-improvement methods (silence trimming, dynamic per-phoneme speed-up, spectral/prosody shaping) used by tools like Overcast Smart Speed and Audacity, distinct from raw time-stretching."}, {"label": "practitioner/implementation — libraries and real-world tools", "query": "audio time stretch library implementation Rubber Band SoundTouch librosa adaptive variable-rate speech playback open source", "rationale": "Provides concrete, codeable implementations and engineering tradeoffs (artifacts, transient handling, variable-rate playback) for actually building a speed-listening / comprehensibility pipeline."}]
input.summary: Decomposition strategy: split the question into (1) the signal-processing techniques that make audio more comprehensible and time-compressible, and (2) the human perception/adaptation science behind extreme speed listening. The five angles cover core TSM algorithms, the psychoacoustic comprehension ceiling, training/adaptation effects (especially blind screen-reader users who reach >16x), comprehensibility-enhancement methods like silence/pause compression and prosody shaping, and practitioner-level libraries/tools for implementation. Together they span theory, evidence, human factors, and buildable engineering.

content.text: The validation failed because the angles need to be at the top level of the structured output, not nested. Let me correct the structure.

input.question:
Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.</parameter>
<parameter name="summary">Decomposition strategy: split the question into (1) the signal-processing techniques that make audio more comprehensible and time-compressible, and (2) the human perception/adaptation science behind extreme speed listening. The five angles cover core TSM algorithms, the psychoacoustic comprehension ceiling, training/adaptation effects (especially blind screen-reader users who reach >16x), comprehensibility-enhancement methods like silence/pause compression and prosody shaping, and practitioner-level libraries/tools for implementation. Together they span theory, evidence, human factors, and buildable engineering.

angles.label: broad/primary — time-scale modification algorithms
angles.query: WSOLA PSOLA phase vocoder time-scale modification speech speed-up algorithm comparison intelligibility
angles.rationale: Surfaces the core DSP techniques named in the question and how they preserve pitch while compressing duration, the foundation of speed-listening playback.

angles.label: academic/technical — perceptual limits of accelerated speech
angles.query: perceptual limits comprehension time-compressed speech maximum word rate intelligibility threshold psychoacoustics study
angles.rationale: Targets peer-reviewed research on where comprehension breaks down (typically cited around 2.5-3x / ~275-400 wpm), grounding the 'limits' part of the question in evidence.

angles.label: training/adaptation effects — blind and trained listeners
angles.query: blind listeners ultra-fast speech screen reader adaptation training 16x 22x time-compressed speech comprehension neuroplasticity
angles.rationale: The >16x claim comes largely from blind power-users of screen readers; this angle finds studies on training, cortical reorganization, and adaptation enabling extreme rates.

angles.label: intelligibility enhancement & silence/pause compression
angles.query: speech intelligibility enhancement silence pause removal spectral prosody processing podcast smart speed dynamic compression
angles.rationale: Covers the comprehensibility-improvement methods (silence trimming, dynamic per-phoneme speed-up, spectral/prosody shaping) used by tools like Overcast Smart Speed and Audacity, distinct from raw time-stretching.

angles.label: practitioner/implementation — libraries and real-world tools
angles.query: audio time stretch library implementation Rubber Band SoundTouch librosa adaptive variable-rate speech playback open source
angles.rationale: Provides concrete, codeable implementations and engineering tradeoffs (artifacts, transient handling, variable-rate playback) for actually building a speed-listening / comprehensibility pipeline.

content.text:
Now I can see two errors: `summary` is reported missing AND `angles` needs at least 3 items. The minimal test had 1 angle. The fact that `summary` is still reported missing even in the minimal case where I clearly passed it strongly suggests the harness is dropping the `summary` parameter — possibly because the parameters are being submitted but the `summary` key isn't registering. 

This looks like a parameter ordering/encoding issue in the harness. The order in the schema is `question`, `angles`, `summary`. Let me try matching that exact order with 3+ angles.

content.text:
Interesting — now `angles` is reported missing instead of `summary`. This is revealing: the harness appears to only successfully register the FIRST one or two parameters and drops later ones. When `summary` was last, it was dropped; now `summary` is second and `angles` is third, so `angles` got dropped.

The pattern: the parameter parsing seems to fail on large multi-line values. The `angles` array is the largest value. Let me try putting `angles` first and making each rationale very short to reduce size, plus minimizing the question.

content.text: The structured output was accepted. The issue was parameter size/ordering in the harness; placing the largest value (`angles`) first allowed all three properties to register successfully.
